prospects.thesis: Hims & Hers is a direct-to-consumer telehealth platform monetizing personalized GLP-1, dermatology, and mental health prescriptions through an AI-driven subscription model with expanding margins. The stock leads the screen on composite score with a blistering +38.1% 1M move, bullish options flow, and institutional accumulation — pointing to continued momentum into Q2 earnings, where subscriber growth and weight-loss category expansion should be the key catalyst.

Vistra is the premier merchant power play on AI datacenter electricity demand, with a nuclear-heavy generation fleet (Comanche Peak) and hyperscaler PPA optionality. With Financials and Comm. Svcs leading the tape but rates still elevated, VST offers rare growth-utility exposure — bullish options, institutional buying, and continued power-price tightness set up a favorable risk/reward.

Datadog is a core observability platform benefitting directly from AI workload proliferation, with recent product launches (LLM Observability, Bits AI) monetizing agentic and inference monitoring. However, at 25.9x P/S with a +15.5% 1M run and 10Y at 4.52%, the multiple is stretched — better to wait for a pullback rather than chase at current levels.

Veeva is the dominant vertical SaaS provider for life sciences (CRM, Vault, clinical data), now leaning into AI-native workflows for pharma R&D and commercial operations. With Healthcare up +2.30% on the week and Veeva showing bullish options plus institutional inflows, the Salesforce CRM migration cycle and AI product cross-sell provide multi-quarter revenue visibility.

NRG is an integrated retail power and generation company gaining from Texas (ERCOT) load growth, datacenter demand, and its Vivint smart-home cross-sell. At just 0.9x P/S and a $29B cap with bullish options and institutional support, NRG offers a cheaper, cash-flow-rich complement to the VST thesis without duplicating the pure-play IPP profile.

market_pulse.rotation_analysis: Big money is loading up on the classic "late-cycle defensive-plus-rate-cut" playbook: Energy (XLE) is getting aggressively bought, which lines up with the administration's "drill baby drill" posture, LNG export approvals restarting, and Middle East risk premium creeping back in — while Utilities and Real Estate going bullish together is a dead giveaway that smart money thinks the Fed is closer to cutting than the headlines suggest. Financials showing unusual bullish volume at 2.7x normal is interesting — that's a bet on deregulation, looser capital rules under the current Treasury, and a steeper yield curve helping bank margins. On the other side, the aggressive hedging in Tech (XLK put/call at 3.64) and Industrials (4.21 with 3.9x volume) tells you institutions are quietly de-risking from the AI trade and worrying that tariffs on China and Mexico will hammer industrial supply chains and capex plans — Healthcare getting sold hard likely reflects drug pricing executive order fears and RFK Jr./HHS policy uncertainty around vaccines and PBMs. Historically, this rotation pattern — defensives and energy up, growth and cyclicals hedged, credit spreads widening, investment-grade bonds getting sold (that LQD put/call of 6.90 is screaming) — precedes a 4-8 week period of choppy-to-down markets where the index masks a lot of pain under the surface. The contradiction worth noting: stocks near highs while credit spreads are widening and IG bonds are being dumped is the market's version of a smoke alarm — equity investors are partying while bond investors are heading for the exits, and bonds are usually right.
